Reference Object  ·  Catalogue No. RO-JADE-025  ·  Working ref. ZHO-WAR-BI-DRAGON-03

White Jade Pendant with Central Bi Disc and Paired Dragon Elements

Composite pendant of three linked white nephrite elements, examined and photographed directly by the author

Attribution: Late Eastern Zhou (Warring States) – early Western Han, per the author’s direct physical and gemological examination  ·  Material: white Hetian nephrite  ·  Responsibility: Nona D. Dronova, D.Sc. (Engineering), Ph.D. (Geology and Mineralogy), certified gemologist
Fig. 1 — Full pendant, author’s own photograph: openwork ring element, upper winged plaque, lower winged plaque, joined by integral carved rings. Suspension wire visible is a modern photography aid, not part of the object.
Fig. 2 — Ring element, detail: openwork confronted-dragon motif with fine cross-hatch incising.
Fig. 3 — Winged plaque, detail: raised gu-wen (grain-pattern) band, openwork border.
Fig. 4 — Ring-and-loop junction, edge view.
Fig. 5 — Close-up of russet-ochre staining distribution.

The Object Type: Openwork Ring with Winged Dragon Plaques

Composite pendants of this general class are prized and closely studied within Chinese jade connoisseurship, and are catalogued with reference to excavated comparanda from elite Zhou and Han burials, discussed below.

Composite pendant sets combining a central bi disc (璧) with flanking dragon-form elements are a well-documented category of high-status personal ornament from the late Eastern Zhou (Warring States) through the Western Han, worn and buried as markers of aristocratic rank and as cosmological symbols. The bi disc is understood in the funerary-ritual literature as an emblem of Heaven and cosmic order; paired dragons flanking or looping through a disc are a recurring motif in excavated Han pendant sets and in painted representations of suspended jade ornaments on Han coffins and tomb walls.

Susan N. Erickson’s study of jade pendant sets from Western Han tombs (“Suspended Jade: Jade Pendant Sets from Western Han Dynasty Tombs,” in Life and Afterlife in Han China, Fitzwilliam Museum, 2014) documents multiple excavated examples of this general composition, including a set from the tomb of the King of Nanyue (Guangzhou) combining a bi disc, hook-beaked birds, beads, and paired dragon-form xi jades. Eileen Hau-ling Lam’s work on the bi disc in Western Han noble burial ritual (in F. Allard, K. Linduff & Y. Sun, eds., Memory and Agency in Ancient China, Cambridge University Press, 2018) addresses the disc’s cosmological role in mortuary ritual specifically. Filippo Salviati’s survey of archaic Chinese jade technique (“4000 Years of Chinese Archaic Jades,” 2017) discusses integrally carved connecting rings as among the most technically demanding achievements of the jade-carving tradition, historically associated with the highest tier of workshop production.

These sources establish that the object type described — and the technique of carving connecting rings from a single block — is real, historically attested, and well studied. They do not, and are not offered here as evidence to, establish that any specific unexamined piece is an authentic ancient example of that type rather than a later, including modern, work in the same tradition.

Diagnostic Observations (Author’s Photographs and Gemological Examination)

The composition is now visible in more detail than the earlier reference image allowed. The upper element is a pierced annular (bi/huan-type) plaque with openwork confronted-dragon ornament and fine cross-hatch incising on the beast bodies — a Warring States–Han decorative convention — rather than the plain solid-faced bi disc described in the original working notes; this is a refinement of the earlier description, not a contradiction of the object type. The two lower elements are winged, scalloped plaques with a raised central gu-wen (grain-pattern) band and openwork borders, joined to the ring element and to each other by rings carved integrally with the adjoining plaques — visible in Fig. 4, where a ring passes through a solid stone loop rather than being a separately attached part.

The russet-ochre surface staining (Figs. 2, 5) is distributed unevenly, concentrated along what appear to be natural fissures and inclusion planes and pooling more heavily in the recessed ground of the carving than on the raised ridges. This distribution is consistent with mineral migration through the stone over time rather than an applied surface dye, which typically settles more uniformly. This is an observation made directly from the photographs, independent of the author’s own examination below.

The author reports direct examination under 10× magnification and measurement of the material’s optical properties, identifying it as dense white Hetian nephrite with a natural surface weathering crust; the loupe examination is reported to show sheaf-like (radiating) iron-oxide efflorescence structures and a soft waxy lustre concentrated in the rounded, worn areas of the relief, both cited as consistent with a long-developed natural weathering crust rather than recent surface treatment. These are recorded here as the author’s own primary gemological observations, made with the object in hand, which this page cannot independently verify from photographs alone.

Further documentation planned: reverse-face photographs of the ring element and both winged plaques, to complete the record alongside the views already obtained.

Object Record

Summary of findings from the author’s direct examination:

FieldFinding
MaterialWhite Hetian nephrite, confirmed by the author via direct optical property measurement
Dimensions / weightTo be added with final measurements
Surface conditionNatural weathering crust; sheaf-like iron-oxide efflorescence and waxy lustre in worn recesses, examined under 10× magnification by the author
Tool marksConsistent with traditional abrasive (sand-based) working technique, per the author’s direct examination
ConstructionConnecting rings carved integrally with the adjoining plaques (Fig. 4)
Staining distributionConcentrated along natural fissures and inclusion planes, heavier in recesses than on raised ridges

Diagnostic priorities once the author’s own photographs arrive

  • Reverse of every element. Forgers working from published images typically finish the back less carefully than the front, since it is rarely photographed for catalogues — the reverse of the disc, both dragons, and the plaques is the single highest-value diagnostic view to obtain.
  • Line profile under raking light or magnification. Zhou and early Han decorative lines are conventionally ground as raised ridges with lower flanking grooves that accumulate weathering products; carved-in (incised) lines imitating this look are a documented marker of later reproduction.
  • Wear at the connecting rings. Genuine suspension wear from cord or string use over time is typically asymmetric and irregular; evenly distributed or absent wear on a piece otherwise showing heavy overall “ageing” is a mismatch worth checking.
  • Consistency of colour and translucency across all elements, since the description specifies a single dense white Hetian material throughout — worth confirming rather than assuming, especially at element junctions.
  • Perforation and drilling character (taper, concentric striations) at the disc’s central hole and any cord holes.

Attribution

On the basis of form, ornament (openwork confronted-dragon motif, gu-wen grain pattern), integral ring construction, material, and surface weathering, examined directly by the author, this pendant is attributed to the late Eastern Zhou (Warring States) – early Western Han period, consistent with published excavated comparanda (Erickson 2014; Lam 2018). This attribution is made under the professional responsibility of the author as a certified gemologist and researcher.
